Library map confirms these functions are in libUbikeyJni.so, so this is app-native code, not just libc. Canary failure almost always indicates a memory-safety bug (out-of-bounds write, use-after-scope, etc.).

The function name Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT suggests parsing/decoding ASN.1 certificate data into an X.509 TBS cert structure. Such parsers are classic sources of exploitable overflows when lengths/fields are not fully validated.

JNI bridge Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage likely takes a certificate blob or key material from Java and calls Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, which then calls Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT. The fuzz harness likely passed malformed input, but that alone doesn’t make it non-realistic: remote certificates/keys are attacker-controlled in many real-world flows (e.g., TLS, smartcard / token interaction, QR, file import).

Even without decompiled code, the evidence (stack canary triggered in ASN.1->X509 conversion code) is strong for a genuine buffer overflow or similar memory corruption vulnerability. This is within app code and reachable from JNI. Unless there is proof it's purely harness (e.g., NULL pointers or impossible sizes), this should be treated as a likely vulnerability.

Impact: stack-based buffer overflow implies potential code execution or at least serious memory corruption when fed crafted ASN.1 input; so severity high. Confidence not maximal because we lack exact code and argument taint tracking, but above 0.6 given the function names and failure mode.

No sign crash is due to allocator/sanitizer quirks or known hardened abort; it's a direct stack canary failure, strong indicator of a vulnerability. There’s no evidence of defensive use of __stack_chk_fail as a guard for logic. Hence classify as vulnerable.

All native frames are in libUbikeyJni.so per lib map, so this is app-level code, not just libc. Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is likely custom or thin wrapper around ASN.1 decoder and is where memcpy is used for DER parsing into internal structs.

Fuzzer entry fuzz_one_input feeds arbitrary data to jGetKeyUsage. In real app, jGetKeyUsage probably operates on certificate/key blobs that may come from smart card, external key, or remote server. Those are attacker-influenced in many realistic threat models (e.g., malicious card or MITM-provided certificate).

Given a memcpy crash in an ASN.1 decoder, typical pattern is computing lengths from encoded DER length fields and using them as memcpy size without full bounds checks against the underlying input buffer, which is a textbook out-of-bounds read/write primitive and often exploitable.

However, I don't have direct decompiled code due to tool error; exact arguments (src/dst/len) and their validation cannot be inspected. So I infer from common patterns and naming rather than concrete disassembly; this reduces confidence somewhat but still points strongly to a vulnerability rather than harness misuse.

The crash is not clearly a harness artifact: stack shows a natural call chain from JNI jGetKeyUsage through CERT_Load and ASN.1 conversion; the harness simply replaces normal Java callers but passes a blob. JNI method is a real exported entry point. There is no sign of intentional abort or explicit fatal check; it reaches __memcpy.

Thus, this is likely a genuine memory-safety bug (out-of-bounds in memcpy during DER/ASN.1 certificate parsing) reachable from a JNI method that will process untrusted certificate/key data in real use. A crash in an OBJECT IDENTIFIER decoder during generic fuzzing is very commonly due to malformed length / index handling (e.g., OOB read/write, integer overflow, bad loop bound) in ASN.1 parsing code.

ASN.1 / DER parsing is notoriously fragile; robust implementations must validate length fields and handle nested structures. A crash here under random fuzz input strongly suggests insufficient bounds checking rather than a deliberate abort.

Nothing in the call stack indicates that the harness is doing impossible things like passing NULL native pointers; instead, input is being treated exactly as an X.509 certificate would be in production (CERT_Load path).

The path is clearly reachable from Java via the JNI method; in a real app this would likely be given certificate or key material from network / NFC / hardware token, i.e., attacker-controllable within protocol context.
Even if not yet proven exploitable, a crash in DER OID decoding on malformed input is at least a reliability / DoS vulnerability, and frequently exploitable memory corruption (CWE-787/125/190).

chain_of_thought: Ghidra MCP endpoints for native analysis returned 404, so no decompilation or disassembly of libUbikeyJni.so is available for this assessment; only the symbolic stack and Java declaration are known.

The native stack shows a crash in Convert_BINT_to_Binary, called from Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, then Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT, then an unknown frame, with the JNI entry being Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage. This strongly suggests custom ASN.1/X.509 parsing and big‑integer handling inside the vendor library.

The exposed JNI method jGetKeyUsage takes a single byte[] (certificate blob) and returns a String; there is no Java‑side validation of the byte[] size or structure, and it is realistic in the app that certificate bytes can originate from untrusted sources (server, file, NFC, etc.). The fuzzer similarly passes arbitrary bytes via the same API.

Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT are likely higher‑level ASN.1/X.509 decoders that parse the input buffer and then populate internal big‑integer representations (BINT) for fields like serial numbers, key parameters, or bit strings like KeyUsage. Convert_BINT_to_Binary then converts those internal big integers to raw binary/bitfields for further interpretation.

A crash in Convert_BINT_to_Binary is highly characteristic of memory safety issues: e.g., it may allocate a buffer based on an assumed (but unchecked) BINT length, or it may iterate over digits without verifying bounds, leading to out‑of‑bounds read/write or integer overflow on bit length. Such bugs are common in custom big‑integer implementations and ASN.1 parsing code.

Because arguments to Convert_BINT_to_Binary ultimately derive from parsing the attacker‑controlled ASN.1 certificate bytes, malformed inputs can easily produce inconsistent lengths or structures. If the parser does not robustly validate length fields and bounds before building BINTs, an attacker can trigger invalid internal state and subsequent unsafe memory operations in Convert_BINT_to_Binary.

The stack trace includes no sanitizers or obvious harness abnormalities (e.g., NULL JNI env, impossible JNI signatures). The app exposes multiple JNI methods that directly accept raw byte arrays without Java checks, supporting the view that this is intended to handle arbitrary external data. The presence of loops in the call stack (cktrace stopped: previous frame identical) simply indicates stack‑unwinding issues, not that this is a benign logical abort.

Without native code we cannot confirm the exact instruction (memcpy, loop, etc.) or prove exploitable out‑of‑bounds access, but the combination of a big‑integer conversion crash, attacker‑controlled ASN.1 input, and typical bug patterns in such code makes a genuine memory‑safety vulnerability more likely than an environment/harness artifact.

Impact‑wise, if Convert_BINT_to_Binary performs unsafe memory access on heap/stack buffers sized from parsed lengths, a crafted certificate could cause heap corruption or controlled out‑of‑bounds writes, plausibly enabling code execution within the app process or at least a robust DoS. Given the limited evidence, we conservatively rate severity as high (memory corruption risk) with medium‑high confidence of real vulnerability rather than harness fault.
reasons: Crash occurs inside custom big‑integer conversion (Convert_BINT_to_Binary) reachable from JNI with attacker‑controlled certificate bytes.
Java layer performs no length or structure validation on the byte[] passed into jGetKeyUsage, so malformed ASN.1 can influence internal BINT state.
Big‑integer and ASN.1 parsing code is historically prone to out‑of‑bounds and integer bugs; no evidence suggests this is a harness‑only misuse or deliberate abort.
recommendations: Reverse‑engineer and audit Convert_BINT_to_Binary, Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT, and Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT for all length and index calculations; add strict bounds checks before buffer allocations and loops.
Add robust ASN.1 certificate validation (length checks, structural validation) before or within JNI code, and reject malformed inputs early instead of processing inconsistent BINT structures.
Instrument libUbikeyJni.so with ASan/ubsan under fuzzing specifically targeting jGetKeyUsage and related certificate APIs to confirm and localize any out‑of‑bounds access or integer overflows.

Convert_BINT_to_Binary operates on buffers or structures whose sizes are derived from parsed ASN.1 length fields without full defensive checks.
The observed termination is due to memory‑safety failure in Convert_BINT_to_Binary rather than an intentional fatal error path.

chain_of_thought: Top native frame is Convert_Binary_to_BINT in libUbikeyJni.so, below that are DER/X509 parsing helpers. Termination reason is generic "Process Termination : Convert_Binary_to_BINT" with no allocator/sanitizer context, so specific corruption type is unknown. However this function name strongly suggests conversion of an arbitrary-length binary blob into a big integer (BINT) structure, typically used when parsing ASN.1 INTEGERs inside certificates or keys.

The native stack shows a clear data-flow: CERT_Load → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_CERT → Convert_ASN1_to_X509_TBS_CERT → Decode_DER_Integer → Convert_Binary_to_BINT. All of these are application crypto/X.509 helpers, not system allocators. There are no obvious harness-only frames except fuzz_one_input at the bottom; the JNI bridge is Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage, which presumably accepts certificate/key material from Java and passes it into CERT_Load.

In a real app, jGetKeyUsage will process certificate data provided either by remote servers, smartcards, or files, all of which are attacker-controlled from the perspective of this library. The fuzz harness replaces that Java caller with fuzz_one_input, but the path through JNI and into CERT_Load and the parsing functions is realistic and the same code would execute on malformed or malicious DER input in production.

Typical failure modes in DER INTEGER → BIGNUM conversions include: (1) trusting length fields without bounds checking and then reading/writing that many bytes; (2) integer overflows when computing the required number of words for the big-int; (3) lack of checks for leading 0x00, negative numbers, or overly large encodings; and (4) stack or heap buffer overflows when converting variable-size input into fixed-size big-int buffers. A crash in Convert_Binary_to_BINT during fuzzing is highly indicative of one of these patterns, i.e., out-of-bounds read/write or similar memory corruption caused by untrusted input lengths or contents.

The stack does not show any harness-specific abuse such as passing NULL where the app never would; instead the functions down the chain have semantic names that clearly expect arbitrary ASN.1-encoded data. Even if the exact root cause is an assertion or abort inside Convert_Binary_to_BINT, that would correspond to an unhandled malformed certificate, leading at least to a reliable local DoS when parsing hostile certificates. More likely it is a classic BigInt length/overflow bug, which is a memory-safety vulnerability.

Because I cannot decompile the actual functions here, I cannot point to a specific memcpy call with tainted length. However the entire conversion pipeline is directly driven by attacker-provided DER, there is no evidence of length or sanity validation between CERT_Load and Convert_Binary_to_BINT, and these custom crypto/ASN.1 stacks are historically very bug-prone. The presence of a dedicated low-level binary→BINT converter that crashes under fuzz input is strong evidence of a genuine, attacker-triggerable flaw rather than an environmental or harness-only issue.

Given the role of big integers in key handling and certificate parsing, any memory corruption here could potentially be exploitable for code execution within the app process, so severity should be considered high. The classification fits an out-of-bounds access / integer overflow during big-integer conversion (CWE-190, CWE-787), even though the precise instruction is unknown.

No signs that the crash is due solely to the fuzz harness; same code would run on malformed certificates in production
Failure in low-level numeric conversion suggests memory-safety or at least unhandled malformed input, giving an easy DoS and potentially exploitable corruption

chain_of_thought: Stack shows __memcpy -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> CERT_Load -> Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage -> fuzz_one_input -> main; first app-level frame below libc is Decode_DER_ASN1_ST, but I cannot actually decompile due to tool 404, so I must infer from names and general patterns.

__memcpy crash in ASN.1 DER decode routines strongly suggests out-of-bounds read/write or length misuse while parsing attacker-controlled certificate data; CERT_Load name implies loading/parsing a cert from bytes provided via JNI, and Decode_DER_ASN1_ST is a decoder for DER ASN.1 structure, commonly length-field driven.

In normal apps, certificate bytes will ultimately come from untrusted sources (network, file, smart card, etc.). The fuzz harness calls Java_com_ubikey_jni_UbikeyJni_jGetKeyUsage directly, meaning its parameters are under fuzzer control and mapped to what in production would be attacker-controlled inputs. There is no indication of the crash being caused by impossible harness-only behavior (like deliberate NULL where app never would), only that malformed certificate data triggered memcpy.

Given function naming, the crash is almost certainly due to using ASN.1 length or size fields to drive memcpy without robust bounds checking against the actual buffer length. That is classic memory corruption (CWE-787/119/125). The fact the crash is at __memcpy (not e.g. explicit abort) and involves a DER decoder further supports a bug in the app/lib code rather than harness.

Without code I cannot prove exact dataflow, but the path CERT_Load -> Decode_DER_ASN1_ST -> __memcpy is a standard pattern for vulnerable ASN.1 parsers. It is realistic that network- or file-supplied certificates reach this code in production, so this is not harness-only. I therefore classify it as a likely real vulnerability with high impact (memory corruption) and reasonably high confidence, though not absolute because I cannot inspect the exact memcpy arguments.
